topic: The least-favorable family of Rosenbaum sensitivity analysis (bridge: matched-study Gamma-sensitivity x Huber-Strassen minimax robust testing): for matched pairs with the alternative separated from the Gamma-null polytope (Q_i(n_i)/Q_i(1) > Gamma_i), the all-tests worst-case power frontier equals the NP frontier of the single extreme-tilt least-favorable pair; for matched sets of size >= 3 the single pair provably fails at high levels (verified level-dependent LF switch, extreme-tilt calibration invalid at alpha=0.7) and is replaced by a level-indexed least-favorable MIXTURE family supported on Q-monotone Gamma-tilts; deliverables: the Rosenbaum validity region A (levels where classical extreme-tilt calibration is minimax-sharp among ALL tests), the design-sensitivity envelope Gamma-tilde* on the common-Gamma ray (ceiling of the statistic-choice program, McNemar-consistent on pairs), and the two-sided strict-improvement region over the Bonferroni-doubled practice (verified strict gap 0.158 at Gamma=2, exact equality at Gamma=1); consumers sensitivitymv/senfm and two-sided sensitivity reporting

open_problems.open_problem: For matched sets of size at least three, what is the level-indexed least-favorable mixture on Q-monotone Gamma-tilts, and at exactly which test levels does it collapse to the classical single extreme tilt?

source_refs.claim: The exact multiple-control sensitivity optimization is generally nonseparable; the paper proves asymptotic separability only for specified rank procedures.
source_refs.author: Joseph L. Gastwirth, Abba M. Krieger, and Paul R. Rosenbaum
source_refs.venue: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, Series B

source_refs.claim: The paper develops closed-form pair-matching sensitivity-value theory; its multiple-control discussion leaves open a pair-like formula because the bounding law lacks a closed form.
source_refs.author: Qingyuan Zhao
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: Tilted sensitivity analysis gives a closed-form limiting worst-case law and design sensitivity with multiple controls, but for a candidate statistic rather than over all tests.
source_refs.author: Colin B. Fogarty

source_refs.claim: A fixed representative least-favorable pair yields the robust NP test under an order-two-capacity condition, with a qualified converse indicating why that condition matters.
source_refs.author: Peter J. Huber and Volker Strassen

open_problems.why_unsolved: With three or more assignments in a matched set, the ordering that maximizes one rejection region need not maximize another, so the adversary can switch laws with the level. Existing work either approximates the nonseparable optimization for a prespecified rank statistic or changes that statistic by tilting; neither identifies the convexified least-favorable distribution for the game over all tests. The missing object is a mixture satisfying level-specific saddle-point and complementary-slackness conditions, not another tail bound for a chosen score.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A finite-dimensional minimax theorem giving the support and weights of the least-favorable mixture at each level alpha, plus necessary-and-sufficient inequalities defining the validity region where it reduces to the single extreme tilt.

writing_exemplar.why: It most clearly frames the pair-versus-multiple-control split, the loss of a closed-form worst-case pattern, and the need for a new matched-set object.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: pair matching has a fixed closed-form worst-case tilt -> multiple controls make the conventional optimizer nonseparable and Gamma-dependent -> tilted fixed-statistic analysis restores tractability -> what level-indexed least-favorable mixture solves the all-tests game?

method_exemplars.technique: Use convex robust-testing duality to construct least-favorable distributions and an NP rule, with capacity inequalities certifying the saddle point.
method_exemplars.why_closest: A level-indexed mixture is the natural convex-dual replacement when the global representative-pair condition fails.

method_exemplars.technique: Decompose the multiple-control program set by set, enumerate local Gamma-extrema, and combine them into asymptotically correct tail bounds and confidence procedures.
method_exemplars.why_closest: Its local programs expose the Q-monotone extreme tilts from which the global least-favorable mixture must be assembled.

method_exemplars.technique: Tilt a score by worst-case assignment probabilities and prove a closed-form limiting worst-case distribution and design-sensitivity formula under multiple controls.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It provides current matched-set limit theory to port once the fixed candidate score is replaced by the minimax rule induced by the mixture.

open_problems.open_problem: For pair-matched Rosenbaum models satisfying Q_i(2)/Q_i(1) > Gamma_i, is the single extreme-tilt pair least favorable uniformly over test levels, and what is the exact all-tests worst-case power frontier?

source_refs.claim: Rosenbaum derives sharp Gamma-sensitivity bounds for named permutation statistics, including Wilcoxon and McNemar-Cox, but does not optimize power over all tests.
source_refs.author: Paul R. Rosenbaum

source_refs.claim: Composite hypotheses described by alternating capacities of order two admit minimax tests that are ordinary NP tests for one fixed representative pair.
source_refs.author: Peter J. Huber and Volker Strassen

open_problems.why_unsolved: The Rosenbaum theory maximizes a tail probability after a statistic has been chosen, whereas the Huber-Strassen conclusion requires a saddle point for the entire test class. It remains to prove that the pair-matched Gamma-null polytope and the separated alternative generate the needed capacity order, and that the familiar extreme tilt simultaneously supports every level constraint rather than only the monotone tail of a prespecified score.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A necessary-and-sufficient saddle-point theorem identifying the extreme-tilt null/alternative pair, its likelihood-ratio test, and the exact minimax power function for every level in a stated validity region.

writing_exemplar.why: Its progression from hidden assignment bias to sharp finite-sample bounds is the natural setup to lift from a fixed statistic to the all-tests frontier.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: matching removes observed imbalance but not bounded hidden assignment bias -> Gamma gives sharp tails for standard pair statistics -> when does the same model admit one least-favorable pair for every valid test?

method_exemplars.technique: Represent lower and upper probabilities by alternating capacities, construct a representative least-favorable pair, and prove minimax equality by the NP lemma and capacity inequalities.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The desired pair result is exactly a robust-testing saddle point with the finite Gamma polytope replacing classical contamination neighborhoods.

method_exemplars.technique: Reduce bounded within-pair treatment odds to extremal Bernoulli assignments and compute sharp finite-sample permutation tails.
method_exemplars.why_closest: This fixed-statistic extreme construction is precisely what an all-tests NP-frontier theorem must recover and strengthen.

open_problems.open_problem: On the common-Gamma ray, what is the maximal design sensitivity over all valid matched-study tests, and can an attaining sequence be characterized that reduces to the McNemar frontier for binary matched pairs?

source_refs.claim: The paper introduces design sensitivity as the asymptotic threshold measuring how designs and analysis strategies distinguish effects from hidden bias.
source_refs.author: Paul R. Rosenbaum

source_refs.claim: The paper shows that ordinary efficiency and design sensitivity rank selected procedures differently, making statistic choice substantive under hidden bias.
source_refs.author: Paul R. Rosenbaum
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: A constructed family of U-statistics can materially improve design sensitivity over Wilcoxon, demonstrating a nontrivial statistic-choice program.
source_refs.author: Paul R. Rosenbaum

source_refs.claim: The paper optimizes within signed-score choices for pair matching and shows that the best finite-sample score depends on the outcome law, tails, and sample size.
source_refs.author: Qingyuan Zhao
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: The paper gives closed-form design sensitivity for tilted procedures with multiple controls and compares candidate procedures, but does not upper-bound all valid tests.
source_refs.author: Colin B. Fogarty

open_problems.why_unsolved: Published design sensitivities are derived or optimized within selected families such as signed scores, U-statistics, coherent combinations, and tilted scores. An upper envelope over every level-valid test is missing because statistic selection and the adversary's least-favorable assignment law must be solved jointly; optimizing a score against the classical extreme tilt is circular when that law can switch with the score or the rejection level in larger matched sets.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A variational formula for the all-tests design-sensitivity envelope, a converse valid for arbitrary test sequences, and a constructive attaining sequence whose binary-pair specialization equals the McNemar-consistent frontier.

writing_exemplar.why: It supplies the canonical argument for judging an observational procedure by separation from hidden bias rather than only randomized-experiment efficiency.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: observational studies must distinguish effects from bounded hidden bias -> design sensitivity measures the large-sample separation threshold -> existing work compares selected statistics -> what is the ceiling over all valid tests?

method_exemplars.technique: Derive a CLT for transformed sensitivity values and turn signed-score selection into optimization of weighted tail probabilities with finite-sample corrections near the boundary.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the closest explicit statistic-choice program for pairs and supplies limit theory for an attaining score once the global upper envelope is known.

method_exemplars.technique: Construct U-statistic kernels concentrating on coherent large responses and prove their design-sensitivity gains over Wilcoxon.
method_exemplars.why_closest: These kernels form a rich concrete subclass against which the all-tests converse and candidate attaining sequence can be tested.

method_exemplars.technique: Couple the statistic to Gamma through tilting, derive its limiting worst-case law, and obtain a closed-form matched-set design sensitivity.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The construction already joins score design to the sensitivity model and is the most direct starting point for an all-tests attaining sequence.

open_problems.open_problem: What is the exact two-sided minimax Rosenbaum sensitivity test, and for which Gamma, level, and alternative regimes does it strictly dominate doubling the smaller separately worst-cased one-sided p-value?

source_refs.claim: After developing one-sided theory, the paper records the standard two-sided sensitivity p-value as twice the smaller of the two one-sided upper bounds.
source_refs.author: Qingyuan Zhao
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: The paper casts multivariate one-sided sensitivity analysis as an adversarial game and optimizes coherent tests, but prespecifies effect directions.
source_refs.author: Colin B. Fogarty

source_refs.claim: Dependence-aware calibration of multiple analyses of one hypothesis can cost much less than a generic multiplicity correction while inheriting the best component design sensitivity.
source_refs.author: Paul R. Rosenbaum

source_refs.claim: The robust NP framework optimizes one rejection region jointly against composite null and alternative classes rather than calibrating separate directional tests.
source_refs.author: Peter J. Huber and Volker Strassen

open_problems.why_unsolved: The doubled-tail practice applies a union bound after maximizing the positive and negative tails separately. Under Gamma sensitivity, both tails are constrained by the same assignment law, and their separate worst-case configurations can be incompatible. Existing adversarial methods are one-sided or combine several prespecified statistics; they do not optimize the joint two-sided rejection region, so the strict-gain region and the symmetry boundary where the gain vanishes remain uncharacterized.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An exact level-alpha minimax two-sided rejection rule, its sharp worst-case power formula, and necessary-and-sufficient inequalities for strict dominance over doubled one-sided calibration, including equality at Gamma equal to one.

writing_exemplar.why: Its one-sided sensitivity-value development and explicit doubled-tail convention provide the cleanest direct pivot to a joint two-sided game.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: one-sided Gamma sensitivity yields tractable worst-case tails -> routine two-sided reporting doubles a separately optimized tail -> the two tails share one hidden-bias law -> what exact joint minimax calibration exploits that coupling?

method_exemplars.technique: Formulate sensitivity testing as a convex analyst-versus-adversary game, optimize coherent evidence against hidden bias, and derive its nonstandard null limit.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The desired two-sided rule likewise requires joint optimization of coupled directional evidence instead of two separate extrema.

method_exemplars.technique: Calibrate correlated component tests from their joint law and prove that the combined procedure inherits the maximum component design sensitivity.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the closest Rosenbaum-model template for replacing Bonferroni by dependence-aware calibration.

method_exemplars.technique: Construct a robust likelihood-ratio rejection region from least-favorable distributions and prove its minimax saddle-point property.
method_exemplars.why_closest: A genuinely two-sided optimum must come from one joint least-favorable game rather than two independently calibrated tail games.

literature_map:
Huber and Strassen (1973, Annals of Statistics) prove that a robust all-tests problem reduces to an NP test for one representative pair only under a strong capacity condition.
Rosenbaum (1987, Biometrika) introduces sharp Gamma bounds for named permutation procedures, including Wilcoxon and McNemar-Cox, but not the game over all tests.
Gastwirth, Krieger, and Rosenbaum (2000, JRSSB) show that multiple-control worst-case optimization is generally nonseparable and prove asymptotic separability for selected rank procedures.
Rosenbaum (2004, Biometrika) defines design sensitivity as the asymptotic threshold separating detectable effects from hidden assignment bias.
Rosenbaum (2007, Biometrics) extends fixed-statistic sensitivity analysis to m-tests, confidence intervals, pairs, and matched sets.
Rosenbaum (2010, JASA) and Rosenbaum (2011, Biometrics) show that ordinary efficiency and Gamma-robustness rank statistics differently and construct higher-design-sensitivity U-statistics.
Rosenbaum (2012, Biometrika) and Rosenbaum (2013, Biometrics) study dependence-aware combination and the impact of multiple controls, reinforcing the pair/set split.
Zhao (2019, JASA; arXiv:1702.03442) derives pair sensitivity-value and power theory, records doubled one-sided bounds as the two-sided convention, and leaves multiple-control closed-form theory unresolved.
Fogarty (2020, Biometrika; arXiv:1812.07728) formulates multivariate one-sided sensitivity analysis as an adversarial game with a nonstandard joint limit.
Fogarty (2020, JASA; arXiv:1609.02112) provides studentized weak-null sensitivity inference for paired studies.
Fogarty (2023, Biometrics; arXiv:1908.07352) proves a validity-versus-sharpness incompatibility for weak nulls in general matched designs that is absent for pairs.
Wu and Li (2023, arXiv:2309.06459) replace a maximum hidden-bias restriction by quantile restrictions, illustrating alternative convex uncertainty classes.
Fogarty (2025, arXiv:2503.09736) obtains closed-form limiting worst-case laws and design sensitivity for tilted fixed-statistic procedures with multiple controls, but not the all-tests game.
Li (2025, Biometrika; arXiv:2411.10623) changes the randomization justification for flexible matching, showing that matching geometry and sensitivity validity interact.
The live tension is pairwise global extreme tilting versus matched-set nonseparability: no published result gives the level-indexed all-tests mixture, the design-sensitivity ceiling, or exact joint two-sided calibration.
prior_proposal_map:
The exact qid doc/research/active/stat_rosenbaum_lf_family_minimax contains only state.json, pipeline.jsonl, and orchestrator/decision_log.jsonl; no proposal, rejected angle, reviewer JSON, or bank README exists yet.
A catalogue-wide indexed scan found no prior CausalSmith source on matched-study Rosenbaum Gamma testing, Huber-Strassen minimax tests, McNemar design sensitivity, extreme Gamma tilts, level-indexed least-favorable mixtures, or Bonferroni-doubled reporting.
The closest sensitivity entry inspected was doc/research/_bank/failed/pid_qte_marginal_sensitivity_v1, whose kernel concerns Tukey-factor CQTE certificates under a marginal sensitivity model rather than randomization-based matched-study testing.
Its two rejected-angle proposals concern CQTE process compatibility and finite-Gamma quantile endpoints; neither supplies the requested least-favorable testing problem.
Its reviewer strengthening_paths concern rank certificates and simultaneous CQTE inference, not matched-set least-favorable laws.
The adjacent doc/research/_bank/downgraded/pid_policy_regret_ch_sensitivity_v1 and its reviews concern policy-regret ambiguity-set calibration, not Rosenbaum test-size or power frontiers.
Keyword-adjacent least-favorable proposals in stat_ate_overlap_decay and other qids concern minimax estimation or different testing models and were excluded.
Track A therefore contributes no topic-specific entries, and cross-track linking produces no source=both records.
